english: sense: the lowest of the three guṇa or psychological qualities of animated beings, सत्त्व, रज, तमस्, virtue, passion, stupidity, acc. to the Brahminical theory, for which however Buddhism has substituted the three moral categories: འདོད་ཆགས་ ˳dod-čágs ('dod chags), ཞེ་སྡང་ źe-sdáṅ (zhe sdang), གཏི་མུག་ γti-mug (gti mug), voluptuousness, anger, inconsiderateness (Köpp. I, 33); γti-mug (gti mug), as for example, is the source of falsehoods told with a pretended good intention, Stg.; the symbol for it is the pig Wdṅ. Note. The philosophical term ma-rig-pa (ma rig pa) is altogether different from γti-mug (gti mug).
Lex. to fall in drops, to drop, to drip.

Ld. *ltiṅ* (lting), bottom, rgyá-mtsoi γtiṅ-dkrugs (rgya mtsho'i gting dkrugs) he turned up the bottom of the sea; γtiṅ-du núb-pa (gting du nub pa) to sink to the bottom Cs.; depth, hence γtiṅ záb-po (gting zab po) Dzl., γtiṅ riṅ-ba (gting ring ba) deep, γtiṅ nyé-ba (gting nye ba) not deep, shallow; rgyá-mtso-bas γtiṅ-záb-bo (rgya mtsho bas gting zab bo) it is deeper than the sea Dzl.; gyáṅ-sa γtiṅ-riṅ-ba (gyang sa gting ring ba) a deep abyss Thgr.; čú-bo γtiṅ-záb-po żig (chu bo gting zab po zhig) a deep river Dzl. ༢༠༢, 1. (in the third line however záb-bo (zab bo) would be the correct reading for záb-po (zab po)); γtiṅ-záb-kyád kru-brgyád-pa (gting zab khyad khru brgyad pa) eight cubits deep (lit. with regard to depth holding eight cubits) Dzl. ༡༢༥, 5; fig. γtiṅ-nas (gting nas) from the bottom of the heart, ṅá-la dád-pa γtiṅ-nas gyis (nga la dad pa gting nas gyis) believe in me with all your heart Mil.; ka-grógs (kha grogs) and γtiṅ-grógs (gting grogs) v. grogs (grogs); ka-dkár-γtiṅ-nág (kha dkar gting nag) white without, and black within (fig.) Mil.; the following passage of Mil.: rgyá-mtso čé-la dpe lóṅ-la ka-γtiṅ-méd-pai sgom čig gyis (rgya mtsho che la dpe long la kha gting med pa'i sgom cig gyis), is not perfectly clear, yet the real sense seems to be: resembling the ocean, be so lost in contemplation, that you do not know any longer a difference between surface and bottom; γtiṅ-rdó (gting rdo) a stone or piece of lead (żá-nyei γtiṅ-rdó (zha nye'i gting rdo) Pth.) fastened to a rope, and used as plummet, as anchor, as a clock-weight, as a means for drowning delinquents etc.; *ču nyóg-po-če-la tiṅ med* (chu nyog po che la ting med) W. a very muddy water has no depth; γtiṅ-čan (gting can) deep, γtiṅ-méd (gting med) shallow Cs.; also fig. deep, reserved, covert, difficult to fathom, to form an opinion of, and the contrary: shallow, superficial; γtiṅ-mi-lóṅ (gting mi long) C. of unknown depth; γtiṅ-dróṅs-pa (gting drongs pa) fathomed, penetrated, ascertained C.

1. to be gathering, of clouds, sprin-puṅ γtíb-pa (sprin phung gtib pa) thick clouds gathering Wdṅ.; bdug-spós sprin-bżin γtib (bdug spos sprin bzhin gtib) incense wafts along like clouds Glr.; mún-pa γtib (mun pa gtib) Lex., col. also *nam-ka tib-tib yod* (nam mkha' thib thib yod) cf. ˳tib-pa ('thib pa). -- 2. sometimes for rdib-pa (rdib pa).

pf. γtugs (gtugs), also btúg-pa (btug pa), cognate to túg-pa (thug pa), 1. to reach, to touch, yí-dam-gyi túgs-kar γtúgs-nas (yi dam gyi thugs kar gtugs nas) putting or pressing (his forehead) against the breast of the image Glr.; mi żig-gi żábs-la mgó-bos γtúg-pa (mi zhig gi zhabs la mgo bos gtug pa), or only żábs-γtug-pa (zhabs gtug pa) to touch as a supplicant a person's feet (or skirt) with the brow, to cast one's self at another's feet, frq.; btug túg-pa daṅ (btug thug pa dang) was explained: when it (the danger) draws quite near Ma.; to overtake, to reach, in f., e.g. mťa (mtha') the end Lex.; to meet with, to join Tar. 172, 14. -- 2. to bring an action against a person, to sue Sch., thus prob. Dzl. ༡༢༢, 3, and Pth. -- 3. = zád-pa (zad pa) to be exhausted, to be consumed(?) Zum. zád-pai γtúgs-pa (zad pa'i gtugs pa).-- Note. Not only γtúg-pa (gtug pa), but also many of the following words have b as well as g for their initial letter, and moreover a corresponding form beginning with t, cf. the same or nearly the same signification.

Sch. also rtun (rtun), col. *gog-tún* (gog tun) (spelling dubious) 1. pestle; there are small ones, like ours, and large ones, in shape of poles, as thick as a man's arm, and about 6 feet long, by means of which the pounding is effected in an excavation made in a rock, called γtun-kúṅ (gtun khung); γtun(-gyis) rdúṅ-ba (gtun (gyis) rdung ba) to pound with a pestle Dzl.; γtún-po (gtun po) mortar Cs.; γtun-bu (gtun bu), γtun-šiṅ (gtun shing) pestle Cs. -- 2. mallet, knocker Dzl.
more frq. btúb-pa (btub pa), = túb-pa (thub pa), to be able, pyir ˳óṅ-du btúb-pa-am (phyir 'ong du btub pa am) shall you really be able to come
